questions.question: Tower A is 90 m tall and Tower B is 140 m tall. They are 100 m apart. A horizontal skywalk connects the floors at 70 m from the bottom of both towers. If a taut rope connects the top of Tower A to the bottom of Tower B, at what distance (in meters) from Tower A will the rope intersect the skywalk?
questions.to_find: distance from Tower A where the rope intersects the skywalk at height 70 m

understand.plain: Two towers of different heights stand 100 m apart. A rope is stretched from the top of the shorter tower to the base of the taller tower, forming a straight line (diagonal). A horizontal skywalk runs at height 70 m. We need to find where the rope crosses the skywalk.

keywords.explain: A horizontal bridge at height 70 m connecting the two towers
keywords.example: Like a bridge mid-air joining two buildings at the same floor level

keywords.explain: A straight line from one point to another — modelled as a line segment
keywords.example: The rope goes from (0, 90) to (100, 0) in coordinate form

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 340 220' width='340' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='220' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><rect x='30' y='25' width='24' height='155' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='270' y='0' width='24' height='180' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='100' x2='294' y2='100' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><line x1='54' y1='25' x2='270' y2='180' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='103' cy='100' r='5' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'/><text x='42' y='20' class='tag ink'>A (90m)</text><text x='262' y='195' class='tag ink'>B (140m)</text><text x='300' y='104' class='sub mut'>70m skywalk</text><text x='92' y='90' class='sub ink'>x = 22.2m</text><text x='120' y='195' class='sub mut'>100m apart</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two towers A (90m) and B (140m) 100m apart with a rope from top of A to base of B crossing the skywalk at height 70m

given.aim: Find the distance from Tower A where the diagonal rope crosses the horizontal skywalk at 70 m

terms.meaning: 90 m tall; top of A is at height 90 m
terms.example: Place A at x = 0: top is at (0, 90)

terms.meaning: B is 100 m away; its bottom is at ground level
terms.example: Place B at x = 100: bottom is at (100, 0)

terms.meaning: Horizontal level at 70 m connecting both towers
terms.example: All points on the skywalk satisfy y = 70
terms.connects: We substitute y = 70 into the rope's equation to find x

given.plan: Write the equation of the line (rope) from (0, 90) to (100, 0). Find x when y = 70. Report x as the distance from Tower A.

steps.title: Set up coordinates
steps.apply: Place Tower A at the origin. Top of A = (0, 90). Bottom of B = (100, 0).
steps.note: The rope is the line segment joining these two points

steps.title: Equation of the rope (line from A-top to B-bottom)
steps.note: This is the straight-line equation of the taut rope

steps.title: Substitute skywalk height y = 70
steps.note: The rope crosses the 70 m skywalk at about 22.22 m from Tower A

questions.formula_note: No standard library formula needed; solved using slope-intercept form of a line and substitution.

questions.question:
Arrange the following three-dimensional objects in the descending order of their volumes:
 i. A cuboid with dimensions 10 cm, 8 cm and height 5 cm
 ii. A cube of side 9 cm
 iii. A cylinder with base radius 7 cm and height 7 cm
 iv. A sphere of radius 7 cm

A. (i), (ii), (iii), (iv)
B. (iv), (iii), (ii), (i)
C. (iii), (iv), (i), (ii)
D. (iv), (iii), (i), (ii)
questions.to_find: descending order of volumes of the four given 3D objects

understand.plain: We compute the volume of each of the four shapes and then rank them from largest to smallest. The sphere and cylinder both use r = 7 cm which gives large volumes, while the cube and cuboid have smaller sizes.

keywords.explain: V = (4/3) pi r^3; grows very fast with radius
V = pi r^2 h; base area times height

understand.visual_alt: Four 3D shapes compared by volume: sphere > cylinder > cube > cuboid

given.aim: Compute the volume of each shape and sort in descending order

terms.meaning: Rectangular box with length 10, width 8, height 5
Both use r = 7 cm; cylinder also has h = 7 cm
terms.connects: The two largest volumes; sphere > cylinder because (4/3) × 343 > 49 × 7

given.plan: Calculate each volume using standard formulas, then rank.

steps.title: Volume of cuboid (i)
steps.note: Smallest of the four

steps.title: Volume of cube (ii)
steps.note: Second smallest

steps.title: Volume of cylinder (iii)
steps.note: Second largest

steps.title: Volume of sphere (iv)
steps.note: Largest of the four; (4/3) × 343 pi > 343 pi

steps.title: Rank descending
steps.note: Matches option B

solution.result: Descending order: (iv) sphere, (iii) cylinder, (ii) cube, (i) cuboid — Option B

questions.formula_note: Standard 3D volume formulas (sphere, cylinder, cube, cuboid) applied directly.

questions.question:
A set of 4 parallel lines intersect with another set of 5 parallel lines. How many parallelograms are formed?

A. 20
B. 48
C. 60
D. 72
questions.to_find: number of parallelograms formed by 4 parallel lines crossing 5 parallel lines

understand.plain: When two sets of parallel lines cross each other, any two lines from the first set and any two lines from the second set form a parallelogram. We count parallelograms by choosing 2 lines from each set, so we use combinations.

keywords.explain: Choosing 2 lines from the first family and 2 from the second gives exactly one parallelogram
keywords.example: Lines L1, L2 and M1, M2 enclose a parallelogram with 4 sides

keywords.explain: Number of ways to choose 2 items from n is n(n-1)/2
keywords.example: C(4,2) = 6 ways to pick 2 from 4 lines; C(5,2) = 10 from 5 lines

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 280 180' width='280' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='280' height='180' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><line x1='30' y1='20' x2='250' y2='80' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='30' y1='50' x2='250' y2='110' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='30' y1='80' x2='250' y2='140' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='30' y1='110' x2='250' y2='170' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='50' y1='10' x2='70' y2='170' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='95' y1='10' x2='115' y2='170' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='140' y1='10' x2='160' y2='170' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='185' y1='10' x2='205' y2='170' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='230' y1='10' x2='250' y2='170' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='8' y='55' class='tag' fill='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'>4</text><text x='255' y='175' class='tag' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'>5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 4 slanted parallel lines (purple) crossed by 5 vertical parallel lines (green) forming a grid of parallelograms

given.aim: Count distinct parallelograms formed by choosing lines from each set

terms.meaning: Any 2 of these form the top and bottom sides of a parallelogram
terms.connects: Contributes a factor of C(4,2) to the count

terms.meaning: Any 2 of these form the left and right sides of a parallelogram
terms.connects: Contributes a factor of C(5,2) to the count

terms.meaning: Each pair from Set 1 can be combined with each pair from Set 2 independently

given.plan: Use the product rule: number of parallelograms = C(4,2) × C(5,2).

steps.title: Choose 2 lines from Set 1 (4 parallel lines)
steps.note: These two lines become the pair of parallel sides in one direction

steps.title: Choose 2 lines from Set 2 (5 parallel lines)
steps.note: These two lines become the pair of parallel sides in the other direction

steps.title: Total parallelograms by multiplication principle
steps.note: Each combination of 2 lines from each set defines exactly one parallelogram

questions.formula_note: Parallelogram count from two sets of parallel lines uses combinations on each set and the product rule.

questions.question:
A 1.5 m tall person is standing at a distance of 3 m from a lamp post. The light from the lamp at the top of the post casts her shadow. The length of the shadow is twice her height. What is the height of the lamp post in meters?

A. 1.5
B. 3
C. 4.5
D. 6

understand.plain: The lamp at the top of the post shines on a person standing 3 m away. The shadow length equals twice the person's height, that is 3 m, so the shadow tip is 3 m beyond the person. Using similar triangles formed by the lamp, the person, and the shadow tip, we find the lamp height.

keywords.explain: The large triangle (lamp top to shadow tip along the ground) and the small triangle (person's head to shadow tip) are similar because both share the shadow-tip angle
keywords.example: Ratio of heights equals ratio of horizontal bases

keywords.explain: The shadow extends along the ground from the person's feet away from the lamp; here shadow = 2 × 1.5 = 3 m
keywords.example: Person at 3 m from post; shadow tip is at 3 + 3 = 6 m from post

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' width='320' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='180' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><line x1='20' y1='160' x2='300' y2='160' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='100' x2='40' y2='160' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='3'/><text x='10' y='130' class='sub ink'>H</text><line x1='130' y1='130' x2='130' y2='160' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='135' y='148' class='sub ink'>1.5m</text><line x1='40' y1='100' x2='220' y2='160' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='55' y='170' class='sub mut'>3m</text><text x='145' y='170' class='sub mut'>3m (shadow)</text><text x='5' y='95' class='sub ink'>lamp</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Lamp post (height H) with person 3m away (height 1.5m) and shadow tip 6m from post; similar triangles give H

given.aim: Find lamp post height H using similar triangles

terms.meaning: 1.5 m tall; stands 3 m from the base of the post
terms.example: Head is at height 1.5 m, feet at ground level at x = 3 m from post
terms.connects: Forms the small triangle with shadow tip

terms.example: Shadow tip is at 3 + 3 = 6 m from the base of the post
terms.connects: Defines the base of the large triangle

terms.meaning: Large triangle: lamp top (0, H) to shadow tip (6, 0); small triangle: head (3, 1.5) to shadow tip (6, 0)
terms.connects: Gives the equation to solve for H

given.plan: Set up the similar-triangle ratio: H/6 = 1.5/3, then solve for H.

steps.title: Locate shadow tip
steps.apply: Shadow tip is at 3 + 3 = 6 m from the lamp post base
steps.note: The shadow extends 3 m beyond the person's feet

steps.title: Apply similar-triangle ratio
steps.formula_raw: H / (distance of shadow tip from post) = person height / shadow length
steps.note: The ray from lamp top to shadow tip passes through the person's head

solution.result: Height of lamp post = 3 m — Option B

questions.formula_note: Similar triangles formed by lamp, person, and shadow tip give a straightforward ratio equation.

questions.question:
A rectangle becomes a square when its length and breadth are reduced by 10 m and 5 m, respectively. During this process, the rectangle loses 650 m^2 of area. What is the area of the original rectangle in square meters?

A. 1125
B. 2250
C. 2924
D. 4000

understand.plain: A rectangle is trimmed on both dimensions until it becomes a square. The amount of area lost tells us the side of the resulting square, and from that we recover the original dimensions. The area loss is not just the product of the reductions — it includes the corner overlap and two strips.

keywords.explain: Removing a strip of width 10 from the length and a strip of width 5 from the breadth; lost area = 10W + 5L - 50 where L and W are original dimensions
After reduction: L - 10 = W - 5, so L = W + 5
keywords.example: If square side s = 40, then L = 50, W = 45

understand.visual_alt: Rectangle with length L and breadth W trimmed to a square by removing 10m from length and 5m from breadth

given.aim: Find original area L × W given that (L-10)(W-5) is a square and area loss = 650 m^2

terms.example: Let square side = s; then L = s + 10, W = s + 5
terms.connects: Constrains the relationship between L and W

given.plan: Express L and W in terms of square side s, use the area-loss equation to find s, then compute LW.

steps.title: Define the square side
steps.apply: Let s = side of resulting square. Then L = s + 10 and W = s + 5.
steps.note: Both dimensions reduce to the same value s, confirming a square

steps.title: Express area loss in terms of s
steps.note: The cross-terms simplify cleanly

steps.title: Solve for s
steps.note: The square has side 40 m

solution.result: Original rectangle area = 2250 m^2 — Option B

questions.formula_note: The area loss when trimming a rectangle to a square is expressed as a linear equation in the square's side length.

questions.question:
The perimeters of a circle, a square and an equilateral triangle are equal. Which one of the following statements is true?

A. The circle has the largest area
B. The square has the largest area
C. The equilateral triangle has the largest area
D. All the three shapes have the same area
questions.answer: A. The circle has the largest area
questions.to_find: which shape has the largest area when all three have the same perimeter

understand.plain: When shapes share the same perimeter (isoperimetric condition), the circle always encloses the greatest area. Among polygons, more sides means more area, so the square beats the triangle. The circle, with infinitely many sides, beats them all.

keywords.explain: Among all closed plane curves with the same perimeter, the circle encloses the maximum area
keywords.example: A circle with perimeter P has area P^2/(4 pi), which exceeds that of any polygon with the same P

keywords.explain: Set perimeter = P for all three. Derive each area in terms of P and compare.
keywords.example: Circle: P = 2 pi r => r = P/(2 pi) => A = pi r^2 = P^2/(4 pi)

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 340 160' width='340' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='160' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><circle cx='60' cy='80' r='48' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='130' y='47' width='60' height='60' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='255,30 295,115 215,115' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='38' y='145' class='sub ink'>Circle</text><text x='130' y='145' class='sub ink'>Square</text><text x='215' y='145' class='sub ink'>Triangle</text><text x='38' y='158' class='sub' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'>largest</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three shapes — circle (largest, shaded), square, equilateral triangle — all with the same perimeter P; circle has the biggest area

given.aim: Show that the circle has the greatest area when perimeter P is fixed

terms.meaning: P = 2 pi r => r = P/(2 pi); area A_c = pi r^2 = P^2/(4 pi)
P = 4s => s = P/4; area A_s = s^2 = P^2/16
P = 3a => a = P/3; area A_t = (sqrt(3)/4) a^2 = (sqrt(3)/36) P^2

given.plan: Express each area in terms of P, then compare numerically or via coefficients.

steps.title: Area of circle in terms of P
steps.note: Derived from P = 2 pi r

steps.title: Area of square in terms of P
steps.note: Derived from P = 4s, A = s^2

steps.title: Area of equilateral triangle in terms of P
steps.note: Derived from P = 3a, A = (sqrt(3)/4) a^2

steps.title: Compare coefficients
steps.apply: A_c > A_s > A_t for any fixed P
steps.note: Circle wins by the isoperimetric inequality

solution.result: The circle has the largest area — Option A

questions.formula_note: The isoperimetric inequality guarantees the circle maximises area for a given perimeter; verified by direct coefficient comparison.
